segments.title: A naval historian's design lens for Battlestar Deadlock
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke uses Battlestar Galactica Deadlock's warships to test naval-history lessons against fictional spacecraft design.
segments.body: The game provides a compact way to ask familiar naval questions about armament, protection, aircraft, and fleet roles. Dr. Clarke is assessing fictional spacecraft rather than claiming that their battles reproduce historical combat. His test is whether their layouts make sense when compared with the accumulated design experience of real navies and with current warship practice. Opening here establishes the historical lens that guides every later criticism of firing arcs, carrier capacity, and fleet balance.

evidence.note: He introduces the game and says he will consider lessons from naval history and current warship design.

segments.title: Manticore corvettes: useful mass, weak firing arcs
segments.summary: The inexpensive Manticore corvette works as a missile-firing base of fire, but its fore-and-aft guns leave dangerous blind areas.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke likes massing Manticores because they are simple, inexpensive, missile-equipped ships that can provide a base of fire. Their low cost makes them a plausible general-purpose unit for a fictional space navy. His objection is geometric: fore-and-aft guns invite an opponent to attack from the beam, where maneuverability and missile capacity cannot compensate for a missing gun arc. He would instead exploit a spacecraft's three-dimensional form with top-and-bottom mounts or weapons able to cover a full circle.

evidence.note: He praises the Manticore's low cost and missile system, then criticizes its fore-and-aft gun coverage.

segments.title: Real warship gunnery aims for all-round coverage
segments.summary: The Adamant's side-mounted guns prompt a comparison with destroyers designed to keep useful firing arcs across much more of the horizon.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that a spacecraft can use top-and-bottom mounting or carefully placed weapons to cover more approaches than a conventional hull. He contrasts the game's blind zones with Tribal-class destroyers, whose fore-and-aft guns retained useful arcs across the opposite broadside, and Battle-class destroyers, whose forward concentration cleared their fields of fire. Real designers and commanders generally resisted leaving a large sector entirely uncovered. The Colonial ships also ask one weapon battery to serve both as the main armament and as a close-in defensive system, combining missions that normally demand different coverage and responsiveness.

evidence.note: He discusses Adamant gun placement and compares it with the firing arcs of Tribal- and Battle-class destroyers.

segments.title: The Ranger cruiser as a fragile missile platform
segments.summary: The Ranger carries a useful missile load, but Dr. Clarke finds it too vulnerable to serve comfortably as a close-action cruiser.
segments.body: Rather than exchanging fire directly, Dr. Clarke keeps the Ranger high and at range, steering broad lazy turns away from enemy guns while it launches missiles. Its double missile load is valuable against major Cylon units, but the hull feels too lightly protected for close action. Once its missiles are exhausted, he commits it only after Manticores and other ships have reduced the dangerous targets. The result is a standoff contributor sheltered by tougher units, not the robust cruiser its class label led him to expect.

evidence.note: He describes keeping Ranger cruisers outside gun range and relying on their missiles because they feel vulnerable.

segments.title: Why a larger battlestar should carry more aircraft
segments.summary: An Artemis and a larger Jupiter battlestar having the same air group makes little sense to Dr. Clarke's carrier-minded view of force design.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats aircraft capacity as a central reason to build a larger capital ship. An Artemis and the larger Jupiter carrying the same number of squadrons therefore looks like a missed force-design opportunity. Unlike historical warships, these spacecraft do not face the beam and draught limits imposed by the Suez or Panama canals, although construction capacity still places some bounds on size. If the setting can support a larger hull and external launch-and-recovery pods, he argues that the Jupiter should gain meaningfully more hangar space rather than duplicate the Artemis air group.

evidence.note: He questions why Artemis and Jupiter battlestars carry the same air group and argues for expanded hangar space.

segments.title: Standoff missiles meet a fighter-heavy battlespace
segments.summary: Long-range missile combat may suit space, but the game's confined engagements and important fighter threat still demand close-in defences.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke accepts that a real space engagement might be fought at extreme range, much like modern naval missile warfare in which weapons are launched long before impact. Deadlock deliberately compresses the action into a confined game space, so its tactical rules must be judged on their own terms. Fighters remain important within those rules, making close defence a core requirement rather than an afterthought. He would distribute anti-fighter weapons across the port, starboard, forward, upper, and lower approaches, leaving friendly fighters to help cover the stern instead of relying on a narrow main-battery arc.

evidence.note: He contrasts long-range space combat with the game's close action and calls for broadly distributed anti-fighter weapons.

segments.title: Four Artemis battlestars expose a force-design imbalance
segments.summary: A battle group of Artemis battlestars is powerful because they have all-round fire, but that strength points to a weakness in the rest of the fleet.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke describes a battle group built around four Artemis battlestars with a pair of Manticores filling the remaining capacity. The formation is extremely effective because the battlestars can engage threats from any direction while bringing fighters and missiles. Its success nevertheless exposes a structural weakness in the game's force design. The first ship with genuinely broad combat coverage should not have to be the principal capital unit, because smaller escorts ought to provide credible all-round fire at lower cost and greater numbers.

evidence.note: He describes fielding four Artemis battlestars and says broad fire coverage should not begin only with the main capital ship.

segments.title: Flak works, but missiles need a safe path through it
segments.summary: The game makes flak a valuable fighter defence, while raising a practical question about routing friendly missiles through a ship's own barrage.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke finds the flak system satisfying because it resembles the area defence he would expect against attacking aircraft or fighters. The game rule that the barrage also destroys friendly missiles creates a deconfliction problem, however. Because the missiles are long-ranged and guided, he argues that fire control should be able to time their launch or route them above, below, or around the active flak zone. The comparison turns a game mechanic into a useful lesson about coordinating defensive fire with a fleet's own outgoing weapons.

evidence.note: He praises flak's battlefield role and argues that missile flight paths could be deconflicted from it.

segments.title: Boarding threats and combat air patrol
segments.summary: Expected Cylon boarding attacks remain manageable when battlestars keep fighter squadrons close for combat air patrol.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke expected Cylon boarding parties to be unusually dangerous because machine attackers should be less constrained by casualties. In his campaign they have instead concentrated on battlestars protected by nearby fighters, making their attacks easier to defeat. By controlling the squadrons directly, he can use two fighter groups to maintain combat air patrol over four mutually supporting battlestars. That arrangement keeps a coherent local defence while freeing ten of the force's twelve squadrons for interception or offensive work.

evidence.note: He discusses Cylon boarding expectations and describes using two fighter squadrons as close combat air patrol for four battlestars.

segments.title: Viper tactics exploit gaps in ship defence
segments.summary: Vipers are inconsistent in the game, but their flanking and rear attacks underline the cost of leaving gaps in a warship's defensive arcs.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke finds the Vipers erratic: they can either overwhelm a target or accomplish little unless watched closely. Their best attacks come when they deliberately approach a flank or the stern. Those maneuvers exploit the same defensive gaps he criticizes in Colonial warships, and he finds equivalent omissions in Cylon designs especially surprising for an artificial intelligence facing a fighter-heavy enemy. The example reinforces his larger point that an advanced force needs layered, all-round defence rather than assuming an attacker will approach its strongest guns.

evidence.note: He describes Vipers' occasional flanking and rear attacks and links their success to gaps in Cylon and Colonial gun coverage.

segments.title: Carrier choices and the Adamant frigate's limitations
segments.summary: The Adamant depends heavily on its fighter squadron, while a battlestar offers the all-round fighting value that makes a separate carrier hard to justify.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke sees little reason to choose a dedicated carrier beside a battlestar if the capital ship is the only genuinely versatile combatant available. A cheaper carrier could make sense in principle, but the game's limited fleet capacity makes every slot compete directly for combat value. The Adamant illustrates the problem because much of its utility comes from the single fighter squadron it brings, while its own forward and rear firepower remains weak. A hangar is valuable, but it should not excuse neglecting the basic defensive arcs of the hull carrying it.

evidence.note: He contrasts carriers with battlestars and revisits the Adamant's dependence on fighters and weak gun coverage.

segments.title: A small gun-placement change could transform the Manticore
segments.summary: Moving the Manticore's lower gun aft could open its firing arc without obstructing the missile bays, showing how a modest layout change can reshape combat value.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke examines the visible Manticore model and locates its forward gun on the underside of the hull. The mount is positioned so that the hull itself blocks much of its aft fire, even though the missile launchers appear farther back underneath the ship. He argues that shifting the gun roughly a third of the hull length aft could preserve missile clearance while opening a much broader field of fire. The example makes the design critique concrete: a small rearrangement, rather than a larger or more expensive weapons fit, could turn a vulnerable sector into near-all-round coverage.

evidence.note: He inspects the Manticore model and explains how moving its lower forward gun aft could clear the missile bays and widen its firing arc.

segments.title: Battlestars should not be the only all-round combatants
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke's final design test is simple: a balanced fleet should not need to field only battlestars to achieve all-round firepower.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke identifies the battlestar as the game's one consistently capable all-round combatant. Requiring a fleet of capital ships to obtain dependable fields of fire is enjoyable in play but unbalanced as force design. A credible navy needs useful combat coverage at more than one size and cost level, rather than a single expensive answer to every tactical problem. He also stresses that this is an early judgment after about thirty hours with the game, leaving open the possibility that later ships or more experience could change his assessment.

evidence.note: He concludes that battlestars should not be the only ships with dependable all-round firepower.
